stem: How many triangles (sets of 3 mutually adjacent vertices) are there in the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: A triangle is chosen by picking any 3 of the \( [[n]] \) vertices, and in \( K_{[[n]]} \) all three connecting edges are present. So the count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts edges \( \binom{[[n]]}{2} \), which are pairs of vertices, not triples.
This counts ordered triples of distinct vertices; each triangle is counted \( 3! = 6 \) times.
This counts all ordered triples with repetition allowed.
This divides the ordered triple count by 2 instead of by \( 3! = 6 \).
This overcounts the triangles by the number of vertices.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: How many complete subgraphs isomorphic to \( K_{[[r]]} \) (sets of \( [[r]] \) mutually adjacent vertices) are there inside the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Every set of \( [[r]] \) vertices in \( K_{[[n]]} \) is mutually adjacent (since ALL edges are present), so each \( [[r]] \)-subset of vertices gives exactly one \( K_{[[r]]} \). The count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses one fewer vertex per clique than required.
This uses one more vertex per clique than required.
This multiplies by \( r! \), as if the vertices within each clique were ordered.
This treats vertices as chosen with order and repetition, rather than as an unordered subset.
This leaves out one vertex from the pool available to be chosen.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: In the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \), a single vertex \( v \) is fixed. How many triangles (sets of 3 mutually adjacent vertices) contain \( v \)?
solution: A triangle through \( v \) is determined by picking 2 of the remaining \( [[n]] - 1 \) vertices to join \( v \) (all such edges exist in \( K_{[[n]]} \)). So the count is \( \binom{[[n]]-1}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to remove \( v \) from the pool before choosing the other 2 vertices.
This picks 3 other vertices instead of 2, forming a 4-vertex set rather than a triangle with \( v \).
This counts ALL triangles in \( K_{[[n]]} \), not just those through \( v \).
This counts single vertices rather than pairs that complete a triangle with \( v \).
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: In the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \), a single edge \( \{u, v\} \) is fixed. How many triangles (sets of 3 mutually adjacent vertices) contain this edge?
solution: A triangle through the edge \( \{u,v\} \) is completed by any third vertex, since it is automatically adjacent to both \( u \) and \( v \) in \( K_{[[n]]} \). There are \( [[n]] - 2 \) choices for that third vertex, so the count is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to exclude both \( u \) and \( v \) from the pool of possible third vertices, excluding only one.
This forgets to exclude \( u \) and \( v \) from the pool at all.
This counts all edges of \( K_{[[n]]} \) rather than triangles through this one fixed edge.
This excludes one vertex too many from the pool of possible third vertices.
This overcounts by 2 the number of valid third vertices.
This overcounts by 3 the number of valid third vertices.
